misconceptionSummary: Students confuse a frequency tree with a probability tree, expecting decimals or fractions at each branch.

visuals.ruleText: A frequency tree shows actual counts — whole numbers, not probabilities or fractions
visuals.subText: Every node is a number of people, items, or events — never a proportion

steps.narration: A frequency tree stores actual counts — whole number frequencies. Unlike a probability tree, the branches hold numbers of people or items, not decimals between zero and one. All nodes are whole numbers that sum to the root total.

lines.text: All leaves sum to the root

steps.narration: The root is the total. Branches split by category. The leaves — the final endpoints — are the smallest sub-groups. Add all the leaves and you get back to the root total.

lines.text: Reading across leaves gives totals for a sub-category
e.g. all who passed regardless of gender: add pass leaves
e.g. all boys regardless of outcome: read the boys node

steps.narration: You can extract information in two ways: read a single branch node for a first-level category total, or add the matching leaves across branches for a second-level category total.

visuals.expression: Sum of all leaves = root total
visuals.label: Frequency tree = actual counts; leaves sum to root

steps.narration: Every leaf is a count. All leaves sum to the root. This self-checking property makes it easy to spot an error — if the leaves don't total the root, a branch value is wrong.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing probabilities (e.g. 0.6) on a frequency tree branch. Frequency trees hold whole-number counts. Each branch holds a number of people or items — never a decimal proportion.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Frequency trees contain counts, not probabilities. Every number in the tree is a whole number of people or events. If you see a decimal, you have switched to a probability tree.
